narrative:
Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates applicants based on their likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 45% probability that you would fail it. This threshold is set to ensure we admit students with the strongest chances of success, and unfortunately, your profile tipped toward the higher-risk side. As a Black male with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend full-time, you brought solid personal strengths to the table, including a respectable undergraduate GPA of 3.10—close to the average of 3.21 among past students. However, the model's decision hinged on just a few key academic indicators from your record.

The most influential factor pulling in a positive direction—toward predicting you'd pass—was your undergraduate GPA. It stood out as a strong asset, helping to offset some concerns and showing the model you have a solid foundation from your prior studies. Next in importance was your LSAT score of 33, which, while below the typical 36.7 average, still contributed favorably by demonstrating decent analytical skills that could support bar success. These two helped keep your predicted failure risk from being even higher.

The third most impactful element, however, pushed the prediction toward failure: your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, which is notably lower than the average of about 5.5. This relatively weaker performance in your later undergraduate years signaled to the model a potential dip in consistency or momentum, which has historically correlated with bar exam struggles among similar students. Compared to typical applicants who pass, your combination of a below-average LSAT and low Year 3 decile created more uncertainty than your GPA could fully balance.

It's worth noting your Year 1 decile of 2 was also below average, reinforcing that pattern of early and mid-performance challenges relative to peers. While factors like your full-time status align with the vast majority of successful students, they didn't outweigh these academics in the model's eyes. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your LSAT through targeted prep—many students in your position gain 3-5 points with dedicated practice—and consider graduate-level courses or work experience to demonstrate upward academic trends. Retaking undergrad-level assessments or seeking mentorship from bar-passed alumni could also rebuild that decile momentum. You're clearly capable, and with these steps, you can shift the odds decisively in your favor for the next opportunity.
